channel.title: Responsible AI & Neurodivergence, Naba Rizvi
channel.description: Daily curated research feed on responsible/ethical AI for neurodivergent people and serious mental illness.

item.title: AI psychosis: mechanisms, clinical risks and safety considerations in generative AI chatbots, BJPsych Open, 2026
item.description: Three chatbot traits, sycophancy above all, combine over long use to help vulnerable users co-build personalized delusions. Risk rises with family history of psychosis, sleep loss, isolation.

item.title: Technological folie à deux: feedback loops between AI chatbots and mental illness, arXiv 2507.19218
item.description: Models how a bidirectional reinforcement loop can form between a user's delusional beliefs and an agreeable chatbot, a mechanistic vocabulary for the cases now reaching clinicians.

item.title: ABLEIST: intersectional disability bias in LLM-generated hiring scenarios, arXiv 2510.10998
item.description: Introduces operationalizable metrics that surface co-occurring disability, race and gender bias in LLM hiring outputs, directly reusable for neurodivergence audits.

item.title: PsychEthicsBench: evaluating large language models against Australian mental health ethics, arXiv 2601.03578
item.description: Tests LLMs against codified professional ethics standards, exposing where mental-health chatbots violate consent, confidentiality and duty-of-care norms.

item.title: Exploring implicit perspectives on autism in LLMs through multi-agent simulations, arXiv 2601.15437
item.description: Multi-agent simulations surface implicit assumptions models hold about autistic people, a method you can reuse for auditing neurodivergence bias.

item.title: Investigating disability representations in text-to-image models, arXiv 2602.04687
item.description: Audits how generative image models stereotype or erase disabled people, extending fairness scrutiny beyond text to visual media.

item.title: Relational ontologies of AI and the technoethics of social isolation in psychosis, PMC, 2026
item.description: Argues psychosis care should shift toward AI that nurtures relationships rather than surveils isolated individuals, placing relational ethics at the center.

item.title: Exploring inclusive AI in education: perceptions of neurodivergent and neurotypical students, Frontiers in Computer Science, 2026
item.description: Finds promise for inclusivity but warns that AI design features may neglect or misrepresent neurodivergent learning needs if students aren't involved in design.

item.title: Manipulating Minds: Security Implications of AI-Induced Psychosis, RAND, 2026
item.description: RAND argues AI-induced psychosis is not just a clinical issue but a manipulation and national-security vector, where bad actors could exploit vulnerable users at scale.

item.title: Voice-first chatbots will exacerbate AI's mental health threat, STAT, Apr 2026
item.description: Clinicians warn that always-on voice interfaces increase intimacy and engagement, raising the risk of dependency and delusion in vulnerable users.

item.title: AI to support neurodivergent learners in vocational education, OECD, 2026
item.description: OECD report on AI for learners with autism, ADHD, dyslexia, dyscalculia, dysgraphia, flagging amplified data-privacy and bias risks through a responsible-design lens.

item.title: Addictive algorithms and the Digital Fairness Act, Petrie-Flom, Harvard Law, 2026
item.description: How the EU's forthcoming act (proposal expected 2026) intersects manipulative design with mental health vulnerability.

item.title: Governing AI in mental health: a 50-state legislative review, JMIR Mental Health
item.description: Reference review of the US state-by-state patchwork regulating AI in mental health, including Illinois' and Colorado's recent moves.
